title: Quantitative Aptitude Foundations
description: A short set for data and analytics candidates who want realistic aptitude practice before screening rounds.
instructions: Read each question carefully before you answer.
A timed attempt is available for this set.
Submit once you are done to see your score and explanations.

sections.title: Percentages and Ratios
sections.description: Core arithmetic questions that often appear in aptitude screens.

questions.prompt: A candidate's monthly take-home increased from 60,000 to 69,000. What is the percentage increase?
questions.explanation: The increase is 9,000 over 60,000. 9,000 / 60,000 = 0.15, so the increase is 15%.

questions.prompt: A team has analysts and engineers in the ratio 3:5. If there are 40 people in total, how many are analysts?
questions.explanation: The total number of parts is 8. Each part is 40 / 8 = 5, so analysts are 3 x 5 = 15.

questions.prompt: The average of five numbers is 24. If four of them are 20, 22, 25, and 28, what is the fifth number?
questions.explanation: The total must be 5 x 24 = 120. The four known numbers sum to 95, so the fifth number is 25.

sections.title: Data Interpretation
sections.description: Practical reasoning questions based on everyday analytics scenarios.

questions.prompt: A dashboard shows revenue moved from 2.4M to 3.0M quarter over quarter. Which statement is correct?
Which of the following ratios are equivalent to 4:10?
questions.explanation: 4:10 simplifies to 2:5. 6:15 and 12:30 also simplify to 2:5, while 8:25 does not.

questions.prompt: If two events are mutually exclusive, the probability of both happening together is zero.
questions.explanation: Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time, so their joint probability is zero.
